(or **early medieval period**), sometimes controversially referred to as the **[Dark Ages](/wiki/Dark_Ages_(historiography) "Dark Ages (historiography)")**, is typically regarded by historians as lasting from the late 5th to the 10th century.[[note 1]](#cite_note-1) They marked the start of the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") of [European history](/wiki/History_of_Europe "History of Europe"), following the [decline](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire") of the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire"), and preceding the [High Middle Ages](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages") (c. 11th to 14th centuries). The alternative term *[late antiquity](/wiki/Late_antiquity#Terminology "Late antiquity")*, for the early part of the period, emphasizes elements of continuity with the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), while *Early Middle Ages* is used to emphasize developments characteristic of the earlier medieval period.

The **Early Middle Ages** (or **early medieval period**), sometimes controversially referred to as the **[Dark Ages](/wiki/Dark_Ages_(historiography) "Dark Ages (historiography)")**, is typically regarded by historians as lasting from the late 5th to the 10th century.[[note 1]](#cite_note-1) They marked the start of the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") of [European history](/wiki/History_of_Europe "History of Europe"), following the [decline](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire") of the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire"), and preceding the [High Middle Ages](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages") (c. 11th to 14th centuries). The alternative term *[late antiquity](/wiki/Late_antiquity#Terminology "Late antiquity")*, for the early part of the period, emphasizes elements of continuity with the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"), while *Early Middle Ages* is used to emphasize developments characteristic of the earlier medieval period.

The period saw a continuation of trends evident since late [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity"), including [population decline](/wiki/Population_decline "Population decline"), especially in urban centres, a decline of trade, [a small rise in average temperatures in the North Atlantic region](/wiki/Medieval_Warm_Period "Medieval Warm Period") and [increased migration](/wiki/Migration_Period "Migration Period"). In the 19th century the Early Middle Ages were often labelled the *Dark Ages*, a characterization based on the relative scarcity of literary and cultural output from this time. The term is rarely used by academics today.[[1]](#cite_note-Mommsen226227-2) The Eastern Roman Empire, or [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), survived, though in the 7th century the [Rashidun Caliphate](/wiki/Rashidun_Caliphate "Rashidun Caliphate") and the [Umayyad Caliphate](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate") conquered the southern part of the Roman territory.

Many of the listed trends reversed later in the period. In 800, the title of *[Emperor](/wiki/Emperor "Emperor")* was revived in Western Europe with [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne"), whose [Carolingian Empire](/wiki/Carolingian_Empire "Carolingian Empire") greatly affected later European social structure, arts, culture, and history. Europe experienced a return to systematic agriculture in the form of the [feudal system](/wiki/Feudal_system "Feudal system"), which adopted such innovations as [three-field planting](/wiki/Three-field_system "Three-field system") and the heavy plough. [Barbarian migration](/wiki/Migration_Period "Migration Period") stabilized in much of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), although the [Viking expansion](/wiki/Viking_expansion "Viking expansion") greatly affected [Northern Europe](/wiki/Northern_Europe "Northern Europe").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Collapse of Rome

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Collapse of Rome")]

Main article: [Fall of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire")

Starting in the 2nd century, various indicators of Roman civilization began to decline, including [urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization"), seaborne commerce, and population. [Archaeologists](/wiki/Archaeologists "Archaeologists") have identified only 40 percent as many [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") shipwrecks from the 3rd century as from the first.[[2]](#cite_note-3) Estimates of the population of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") during the period from 150 to 400 suggest a fall from 65 million to 50 million, a decline of more than 20 percent. Some scholars have connected this de-population to the [Dark Ages Cold Period](/wiki/Late_Antique_Little_Ice_Age "Late Antique Little Ice Age") (300–700), when a decrease in global temperatures impaired agricultural yields.[[3]](#cite_note-beberglund-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/2008-05-17-SuttonHoo.jpg/250px-2008-05-17-SuttonHoo.jpg)](/wiki/File:2008-05-17-SuttonHoo.jpg)

Replica of the [Sutton Hoo helmet](/wiki/Sutton_Hoo_helmet "Sutton Hoo helmet"); the original was buried with an [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons") leader, probably [King](/wiki/List_of_monarchs_of_East_Anglia "List of monarchs of East Anglia") [Rædwald of East Anglia](/wiki/R%C3%A6dwald_of_East_Anglia "Rædwald of East Anglia"), c. 620–625 CE.[[5]](#cite_note-6)

Early in the 3rd century, [Germanic peoples](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") migrated south from [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") and reached the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea"), creating formidable confederations which opposed the local [Sarmatians](/wiki/Sarmatians "Sarmatians"). In [Dacia](/wiki/Dacia "Dacia") (present-day Romania) and on the steppes north of the Black Sea the [Goths](/wiki/Goths "Goths"), a Germanic people, established at least two kingdoms: [Therving](/wiki/Thervings "Thervings") and [Greuthung](/wiki/Greuthungs "Greuthungs").[[6]](#cite_note-7)

The arrival of the [Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns") in 372–375 ended the history of these kingdoms. The Huns, a confederation of central Asian tribes, founded an empire. They had mastered the difficult art of shooting composite [recurve](/wiki/Recurve_bow "Recurve bow") [bows](/wiki/Bow_(weapon) "Bow (weapon)") from horseback. The Goths sought refuge in Roman territory (376), agreeing to enter the Empire as unarmed settlers. However, many bribed the Danube border-guards into allowing them to bring their weapons.

The discipline and organization of a [Roman legion](/wiki/Roman_legion "Roman legion") made it a superb fighting unit. The Romans preferred infantry to cavalry because infantry could be trained to retain the formation in combat, while cavalry tended to scatter when faced with opposition. While a barbarian army could be raised and inspired by the promise of plunder, the legions required a central government and taxation to pay for salaries, constant training, equipment, and food. The decline in agricultural and economic activity reduced the empire's taxable income and thus its ability to maintain a professional army to defend itself from external threats.

The Barbarians' Invasions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Invasions_of_the_Roman_Empire_1.png/330px-Invasions_of_the_Roman_Empire_1.png)](/wiki/File:Invasions_of_the_Roman_Empire_1.png)

The destruction of the Gothic kingdoms by the [Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns") in 372–375 triggered the Germanic migrations of the 5th century. The [Visigoths](/wiki/Visigoths "Visigoths") captured and looted the city of Rome in 410; the [Vandals](/wiki/Vandals "Vandals") followed suit in 455

In the [Gothic War (376–382)](/wiki/Gothic_War_(376%E2%80%93382) "Gothic War (376–382)"), the Goths revolted and confronted the [main Roman army](/wiki/Late_Roman_army "Late Roman army") in the [Battle of Adrianople](/wiki/Battle_of_Adrianople "Battle of Adrianople") (378). By this time, the distinction in the Roman army between Roman regulars and barbarian [auxiliaries](/wiki/Auxilia "Auxilia") had broken down, and the Roman army was composed mainly of barbarians and soldiers recruited for a single campaign. The general decline in discipline also led to the use of smaller shields and lighter weaponry.[[7]](#cite_note-Eisenberg-8) Not wanting to share the glory, Eastern Emperor [Valens](/wiki/Valens "Valens") ordered an attack on the [Therving](/wiki/Thervings "Thervings") infantry under [Fritigern](/wiki/Fritigern "Fritigern") without waiting for Western Emperor [Gratian](/wiki/Gratian "Gratian"), who was on the way with reinforcements. While the Romans were fully engaged, the Greuthung cavalry arrived. Only one-third of the Roman army managed to escape. This represented the most shattering defeat that the Romans had suffered since the [Battle of Cannae](/wiki/Battle_of_Cannae "Battle of Cannae") (216 BC), according to the Roman military writer [Ammianus Marcellinus](/wiki/Ammianus_Marcellinus "Ammianus Marcellinus").[[8]](#cite_note-9) The core army of the Eastern Roman Empire was destroyed, Valens was killed, and the Goths were freed to lay waste to the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans"), including the armories along the Danube. As [Edward Gibbon](/wiki/Edward_Gibbon "Edward Gibbon") comments, "The Romans, who so coolly and so concisely mention the acts of *justice* which were exercised by the legions, reserve their compassion and their eloquence for their own sufferings, when the provinces were invaded and desolated by the arms of the successful Barbarians."[[9]](#cite_note-10)

The empire lacked the resources, and perhaps the will, to reconstruct the professional mobile army destroyed at Adrianople, so it had to rely on barbarian armies to fight for it. The [Eastern Roman Empire](/wiki/Eastern_Roman_Empire "Eastern Roman Empire") succeeded in buying off the Goths with tribute. The [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire") proved less fortunate. [Stilicho](/wiki/Stilicho "Stilicho"), the western empire's half-Vandal military commander, stripped the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine") frontier of troops to fend off invasions of Italy by the [Visigoths](/wiki/Visigoths "Visigoths") in 402–03 and by other Goths in 406–07.

Fleeing before the advance of the [Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns"), the [Vandals](/wiki/Vandals "Vandals"), [Suebi](/wiki/Suebi "Suebi"), and [Alans](/wiki/Alans "Alans") launched an attack across the frozen Rhine near [Mainz](/wiki/Mainz "Mainz"); on 31 December 406, the frontier gave way and these tribes surged into [Roman Gaul](/wiki/Roman_Gaul "Roman Gaul"). There soon followed the [Burgundians](/wiki/Burgundians "Burgundians") and bands of the [Alamanni](/wiki/Alamanni "Alamanni"). In the fit of anti-barbarian hysteria which followed, the Western Roman Emperor [Honorius](/wiki/Honorius_(emperor) "Honorius (emperor)") had Stilicho summarily beheaded (408). Stilicho submitted his neck, "with a firmness not unworthy of the [last of the Roman generals](/wiki/Last_of_the_Romans "Last of the Romans")", wrote Gibbon. Honorius was left with only worthless courtiers to advise him. In 410, the Visigoths led by [Alaric I](/wiki/Alaric_I "Alaric I") [captured the city of Rome](/wiki/Sack_of_Rome_(410) "Sack of Rome (410)") and for three days fire and slaughter ensued as bodies filled the streets, palaces were stripped of their valuables, and the invaders interrogated and tortured those citizens thought to have hidden wealth. As newly converted Christians, the Goths respected church property, but those who found sanctuary in the [Vatican](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City") and in other churches were the fortunate few.

Migration Period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Mausoleum_of_Theoderic.JPG/250px-Mausoleum_of_Theoderic.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mausoleum_of_Theoderic.JPG)

The [Mausoleum of Theodoric](/wiki/Mausoleum_of_Theodoric "Mausoleum of Theodoric") in [Ravenna](/wiki/Ravenna "Ravenna") is the only extant example of [Ostrogothic](/wiki/Ostrogoth "Ostrogoth") architecture.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Reino_de_los_visigodos-en.svg/250px-Reino_de_los_visigodos-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Reino_de_los_visigodos-en.svg)

Around 500, the [Visigoths](/wiki/Visigoths "Visigoths") ruled large parts of what is now France, Spain, Andorra and Portugal.

The Goths and Vandals were only the first of many bands of peoples that flooded [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") in the absence of administrative governance. Some[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] lived only for war and pillage and disdained Roman ways. Other peoples[[10]](#cite_note-11) had been in prolonged contact with the Roman civilization, and were, to a certain degree, romanized. "A poor Roman plays the Goth, a rich Goth the Roman," said King [Theoderic](/wiki/Theoderic_the_Great "Theoderic the Great") of the Ostrogoths.[[11]](#cite_note-12) The subjects of the Roman empire were a mixture of [Roman Christian](/wiki/State_church_of_the_Roman_Empire "State church of the Roman Empire"), [Arian Christian](/wiki/Arianism "Arianism"), [Nestorian Christian](/wiki/Nestorianism "Nestorianism"), and [pagan](/wiki/Paganism "Paganism").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The Germanic peoples knew little of cities, money, or writing, and were mostly pagan, though they were increasingly converting to [Arianism](/wiki/Arianism "Arianism"), a [non-trinitarian](/wiki/Nontrinitarianism "Nontrinitarianism") form of Christianity that considers God the Son to have been created by, and thus inferior to, God the Father, rather than the two being [co-eternal](/wiki/Pre-existence_of_Christ "Pre-existence of Christ"), which is the position of [Chalcedonian Christianity](/wiki/Chalcedonian_Christianity "Chalcedonian Christianity"). Arianism found some favour in the Roman Empire before being eclipsed by the Chalcedonian position and then suppressed as heretical.

During the migrations, or *[Völkerwanderung](/wiki/V%C3%B6lkerwanderung "Völkerwanderung")* (wandering of the peoples), the earlier settled populations were sometimes left intact though usually partially or entirely displaced. [Roman culture](/wiki/Culture_of_ancient_Rome "Culture of ancient Rome") north of the [Po River](/wiki/Po_River "Po River") was almost entirely displaced by the migrations. Whereas the peoples of France, Italy, Spain and Portugal continued to speak the dialects of [Vulgar Latin](/wiki/Vulgar_Latin "Vulgar Latin") that today constitute the [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages"), the language of the smaller Roman-era population of what is now England disappeared with barely a trace in the territories settled by the Anglo-Saxons, although the Brittanic kingdoms of the west remained [Brythonic](/wiki/British_language_(Celtic) "British language (Celtic)") speakers. The new peoples greatly altered established society, including law, culture, religion, and patterns of property ownership.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Tr%C3%A9sor_de_Gourdon_02.JPG/330px-Tr%C3%A9sor_de_Gourdon_02.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tr%C3%A9sor_de_Gourdon_02.JPG)

A [paten](/wiki/Paten "Paten") from the [Treasure of Gourdon](/wiki/Treasure_of_Gourdon "Treasure of Gourdon"), found at [Gourdon, Saône-et-Loire](/wiki/Gourdon,_Sa%C3%B4ne-et-Loire "Gourdon, Saône-et-Loire"), France.

The *[pax Romana](/wiki/Pax_Romana "Pax Romana")* had provided safe conditions for trade and manufacture, and a unified cultural and educational milieu of far-ranging connections. As this was lost, it was replaced by the rule of local potentates, sometimes members of the established Romanized ruling elite, sometimes new lords of alien culture. In [Aquitania](/wiki/Aquitania "Aquitania"), [Gallia Narbonensis](/wiki/Gallia_Narbonensis "Gallia Narbonensis"), southern Italy and Sicily, [Baetica](/wiki/Baetica "Baetica") or southern [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), and the Iberian Mediterranean coast, Roman culture lasted until the 6th or 7th centuries.

The gradual breakdown and transformation of economic and social linkages and infrastructure resulted in increasingly localized outlooks. This breakdown was often fast and dramatic as it became unsafe to travel or carry goods over any distance; there was a consequent collapse in trade and manufacture for export. Major industries that depended on trade, such as large-scale pottery manufacture, vanished almost overnight in places like Britain. [Tintagel](/wiki/Tintagel "Tintagel") in [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall"), as well as several other centres, managed to obtain supplies of Mediterranean luxury goods well into the 6th century, but then lost their trading links. Administrative, educational and military infrastructure quickly vanished, and the loss of the established *[cursus honorum](/wiki/Cursus_honorum "Cursus honorum")* led to the collapse of the schools and to a rise of illiteracy even among the leadership. The careers of [Cassiodorus](/wiki/Cassiodorus "Cassiodorus") (died c. 585) at the beginning of this period and of [Alcuin of York](/wiki/Alcuin_of_York "Alcuin of York") (died 804) at its close were founded alike on their valued literacy. For the formerly Roman area, there was another 20 per cent decline in population between 400 and 600, or a one-third decline for 150–600.[[12]](#cite_note-13) In the 8th century, the volume of trade reached its lowest level. The very small number of [shipwrecks](/wiki/Shipwreck "Shipwreck") found that dated from the 8th century supports this (which represents less than 2 per cent of the number of shipwrecks dated from the 1st century). There was also reforestation and a retreat of agriculture centred around 500.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Romans had practiced [two-field agriculture](/wiki/Crop_rotation "Crop rotation"), with a crop grown in one field and the other left fallow and ploughed under to eliminate weeds. Systematic agriculture largely disappeared and yields declined. It is estimated that the [Plague of Justinian](/wiki/Plague_of_Justinian "Plague of Justinian") which began in 541 and recurred periodically for 150 years thereafter killed as many as 100 million people across the world.[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15) Some historians such as Josiah C. Russell (1958) have suggested a total European population loss of 50 to 60 per cent between 541 and 700.[[15]](#cite_note-16) After the year 750, major epidemic diseases did not appear again in Europe until the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death") of the 14th century. The disease [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox"), which was eradicated in the late 20th century, did not definitively enter [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") until about 581 when Bishop [Gregory of Tours](/wiki/Gregory_of_Tours "Gregory of Tours") provided an eyewitness account that describes the characteristic findings of smallpox.[[16]](#cite_note-17) Waves of [epidemics](/wiki/List_of_epidemics "List of epidemics") wiped out large rural populations.[[17]](#cite_note-18) Most of the details about the epidemics are lost, probably due to the scarcity of surviving written records.

For almost a thousand years, [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") was the most politically important, richest and largest city in Europe.[[18]](#cite_note-19) Around 100 AD, it had a population of about 450,000,[[19]](#cite_note-20) and declined to a mere 20,000 during the Early Middle Ages, reducing the sprawling city to groups of inhabited buildings interspersed among large areas of ruins and vegetation.

### Eastern Roman Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Eastern Roman Empire")]

Main article: [Eastern Roman Empire](/wiki/Eastern_Roman_Empire "Eastern Roman Empire")

Byzantine Empire

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Justinien_527-565.svg/330px-Justinien_527-565.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Justinien_527-565.svg)

**[Byzantine Empire under the Justinian dynasty](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire_under_the_Justinian_dynasty "Byzantine Empire under the Justinian dynasty")**

* Under Emperor [Justinian](/wiki/Justinian "Justinian") (r. 527–565), the [Byzantines](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") were able to reestablish Roman rule in Italy and most of North Africa.

---

* Justinian's conquests
* [Eastern Empire](/wiki/Eastern_Roman_Empire "Eastern Roman Empire")

The death of [Theodosius I](/wiki/Theodosius_I "Theodosius I") in 395 was followed by the division of the empire between his two sons. The [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire") disintegrated into a mosaic of warring Germanic kingdoms in the 5th century, effectively making the [Eastern Roman Empire](/wiki/Eastern_Roman_Empire "Eastern Roman Empire") in Constantinople the [Greek-speaking](/wiki/Medieval_Greek "Medieval Greek") successor to the classical Roman Empire. The inhabitants continued to regard themselves as Romans, or *Romaioi*, until the [fall of Constantinople](/wiki/Fall_of_Constantinople "Fall of Constantinople") to the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") in 1453. Despite this, to distinguish it from its predominantly [Latin-speaking](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language") predecessor, historians began referring to the empire as "Byzantine", after the original name of Constantinople, [Byzantium](/wiki/Byzantium "Byzantium").

The Eastern Roman or "Byzantine" Empire aimed to retain control of the trade routes between Europe and the Orient, which made the Empire the richest polity in medieval Europe. Making use of their sophisticated warfare and superior diplomacy, the Byzantines managed to fend off assaults by the migrating barbarians. Their dreams of subduing the Western potentates briefly materialized during the reign of [Justinian I](/wiki/Justinian_I "Justinian I") in 527–565. Not only did Justinian restore some western territories to the Roman Empire, including Rome and the Italian peninsula itself, but he also codified [Roman law](/wiki/Roman_law "Roman law") (with [his codification](/wiki/Corpus_Juris_Civilis "Corpus Juris Civilis") remaining in force in many areas of Europe until the 19th century) and commissioned the building of the largest and most architecturally advanced edifice of the Early Middle Ages, the [Hagia Sophia](/wiki/Hagia_Sophia "Hagia Sophia"). However, his reign also saw the outbreak of a [bubonic plague](/wiki/Bubonic_plague "Bubonic plague") [pandemic](/wiki/Pandemic "Pandemic"),[[20]](#cite_note-Harbeck,_et_al.-21)[[21]](#cite_note-Bos-22) now known retroactively as the [Plague of Justinian](/wiki/Plague_of_Justinian "Plague of Justinian"). The Emperor himself was afflicted, and within the span of less than a year, an estimated 200,000 Constantinopolites—two out of every five city residents—had died of the disease.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Theodora_mosaik_ravenna.jpg/250px-Theodora_mosaik_ravenna.jpg)](/wiki/File:Theodora_mosaik_ravenna.jpg)

[Theodora](/wiki/Theodora_(6th_century) "Theodora (6th century)"), [Justinian](/wiki/Justinian_I "Justinian I")'s wife, and her retinue[[23]](#cite_note-24)

Justinian's successors [Maurice](/wiki/Maurice_(emperor) "Maurice (emperor)") and [Heraclius](/wiki/Heraclius "Heraclius") confronted invasions by the [Avar](/wiki/Avars_(Carpathians) "Avars (Carpathians)") and [Slavic](/wiki/Slavic_peoples "Slavic peoples") tribes. After the devastations by the Slavs and the Avars, large areas of the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") became depopulated. In 626 Constantinople, by far the largest city of early medieval Europe, [withstood a combined siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Constantinople_(626) "Siege of Constantinople (626)") by Avars and Persians. Within several decades, Heraclius completed a holy war against the Persians, taking their capital and having a [Sassanid](/wiki/Sassanid "Sassanid") monarch assassinated. Yet Heraclius lived to see his spectacular success undone by the [Muslim conquests](/wiki/Early_Muslim_conquests "Early Muslim conquests") of [Syria](/wiki/Syria_(Roman_province) "Syria (Roman province)"), three [Palaestina provinces](/wiki/Palestine_(region)#Middle_Ages "Palestine (region)"), [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt_(Roman_province) "Egypt (Roman province)"), and [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa_during_Antiquity#Vandals_and_Byzantines "North Africa during Antiquity") which was considerably facilitated by religious disunity and the proliferation of heretical movements (notably [Monophysitism](/wiki/Monophysitism "Monophysitism") and [Nestorianism](/wiki/Nestorianism "Nestorianism")) in the areas converted to Islam.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Walls_of_Constantinople.JPG/330px-Walls_of_Constantinople.JPG)](/wiki/File:Walls_of_Constantinople.JPG)

Restored [Walls of Constantinople](/wiki/Walls_of_Constantinople "Walls of Constantinople")

Although Heraclius's successors managed to salvage [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") from two [Arab sieges](/wiki/Sieges_of_Constantinople "Sieges of Constantinople") (in 674–77 and 717), the empire of the 8th and early 9th century was rocked by the great [Iconoclastic Controversy](/wiki/Iconoclastic_Controversy "Iconoclastic Controversy"), punctuated by dynastic struggles between various factions at court. The [Bulgar](/wiki/Bulgars "Bulgars") and [Slavic](/wiki/Slavic_peoples "Slavic peoples") tribes profited from these disorders and invaded [Illyria](/wiki/Illyria "Illyria"), [Thrace](/wiki/Thrace "Thrace") and even [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"). After the decisive victory at [Ongala](/wiki/Battle_of_Ongala "Battle of Ongala") in 680 the armies of the Bulgars and Slavs advanced to the south of the Balkan mountains, defeating again the Byzantines who were then forced to sign a humiliating peace treaty which acknowledged the establishment of the [First Bulgarian Empire](/wiki/First_Bulgarian_Empire "First Bulgarian Empire") on the borders of the Empire.

To counter these threats a new system of administration was introduced. The regional civil and military administration were combined in the hands of a general, or strategos. A [theme](/wiki/Theme_(Byzantine_district) "Theme (Byzantine district)"), which formerly denoted a subdivision of the Byzantine army, came to refer to a region governed by a strategos. The reform led to the emergence of great landed families which controlled the regional military and often pressed their claims to the throne (see [Bardas Phocas](/wiki/Bardas_Phokas_the_Elder "Bardas Phokas the Elder") and [Bardas Sklerus](/wiki/Bardas_Sklerus "Bardas Sklerus") for characteristic examples).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Porphyrogenetus.jpg/250px-Porphyrogenetus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Porphyrogenetus.jpg)

*Christ crowning [Constantine VII](/wiki/Constantine_VII "Constantine VII")*  
ivory plaque, ca. 945

By the early 8th century, notwithstanding the shrinking territory of the empire, Constantinople remained the largest and the wealthiest city west of [China](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty"), comparable only to Sassanid [Ctesiphon](/wiki/Ctesiphon "Ctesiphon"), and later [Abbasid](/wiki/Abbasid "Abbasid") [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad"). The population of the imperial capital fluctuated between 300,000 and 400,000 as the emperors undertook measures to restrain its growth. The only other large Christian cities were Rome (50,000) and [Thessalonica](/wiki/Thessaloniki "Thessaloniki") (30,000).[[24]](#cite_note-25) Even before the 8th century was out, the Farmer's Law signalled the resurrection of agricultural technologies in the Roman Empire. As the 2006 *[Encyclopædia Britannica](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica "Encyclopædia Britannica")* noted, "the technological base of Byzantine society was more advanced than that of contemporary western Europe: iron tools could be found in the villages; water mills dotted the landscape; and field-sown beans provided a diet rich in protein".[[25]](#cite_note-26)

The ascension of the [Macedonian dynasty](/wiki/Macedonian_dynasty "Macedonian dynasty") in 867 marked the end of the period of political and religious turmoil and introduced a new golden age of the empire. While the talented generals such as [Nicephorus Phocas](/wiki/Nikephoros_Phokas_the_Elder "Nikephoros Phokas the Elder") expanded the frontiers, the Macedonian emperors (such as [Leo the Wise](/wiki/Leo_the_Wise "Leo the Wise") and [Constantine VII](/wiki/Constantine_VII "Constantine VII")) presided over the cultural flowering in Constantinople, known as the [Macedonian Renaissance](/wiki/Macedonian_Renaissance "Macedonian Renaissance"). The enlightened Macedonian rulers scorned the rulers of Western Europe as illiterate barbarians and maintained a nominal claim to rule over the West. Although this fiction had been exploded with the coronation of [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne") in Rome (800), the Byzantine rulers did not treat their Western counterparts as equals. Generally, they had little interest in political and economic developments in the barbarian (from their point of view) West.

Against this economic background the culture and the imperial traditions of the Eastern Roman Empire attracted its northern neighbours—Slavs, Bulgars, and Khazars—to [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople"), in search of either pillage or enlightenment. The movement of the Germanic tribes to the south triggered the great migration of the [Slavs](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs"), who occupied the vacated territories. In the 7th century, they moved westward to the [Elbe](/wiki/Elbe "Elbe"), southward to the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube") and eastward to the [Dnieper](/wiki/Dnieper "Dnieper"). By the 9th century, the Slavs had expanded into sparsely inhabited territories to the south and east from these natural frontiers, peacefully assimilating the indigenous [Illyrian](/wiki/Illyrians "Illyrians") and [Finnic](/wiki/Finnic_peoples "Finnic peoples") populations.

### Rise of Islam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Rise of Islam")]

632–750

Main articles: [Spread of Islam](/wiki/Spread_of_Islam "Spread of Islam"), [Early Muslim conquests](/wiki/Early_Muslim_conquests "Early Muslim conquests"), [Arab–Byzantine wars](/wiki/Arab%E2%80%93Byzantine_wars "Arab–Byzantine wars"), [Muslim conquest of the Levant](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_the_Levant "Muslim conquest of the Levant"), [Umayyad conquest of Spain](/wiki/Umayyad_conquest_of_Spain "Umayyad conquest of Spain"), and [History of Islam in southern Italy](/wiki/History_of_Islam_in_southern_Italy "History of Islam in southern Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Europe_around_650.jpg/500px-Europe_around_650.jpg)](/wiki/File:Europe_around_650.jpg)

Europe around 650

From the 7th century, [Byzantine history](/wiki/History_of_the_Byzantine_Empire "History of the Byzantine Empire") was greatly affected by the rise of Islam and the [Caliphates](/wiki/Caliphates "Caliphates"). [Muslim Arabs](/wiki/Arab_Muslims "Arab Muslims") first invaded historically Roman territory under [Abū Bakr](/wiki/Ab%C5%AB_Bakr "Abū Bakr"), first Caliph of the [Rashidun Caliphate](/wiki/Rashidun_Caliphate "Rashidun Caliphate"), who entered [Roman Syria](/wiki/Roman_Syria "Roman Syria") and [Roman Mesopotamia](/wiki/Roman_Mesopotamia "Roman Mesopotamia"). The Byzantines and neighbouring Persian [Sasanids](/wiki/Sasanids "Sasanids") had been severely weakened by a long succession of [Byzantine–Sasanian wars](/wiki/Byzantine%E2%80%93Sasanian_wars "Byzantine–Sasanian wars"), especially the climactic [Byzantine–Sasanian War of 602–628](/wiki/Byzantine%E2%80%93Sasanian_War_of_602%E2%80%93628 "Byzantine–Sasanian War of 602–628"). Under [Umar](/wiki/Umar "Umar"), the second Caliph, the Muslims decisively conquered [Syria](/wiki/Roman_Syria "Roman Syria") and [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia_(Roman_province) "Mesopotamia (Roman province)"), as well as [Roman Palestine](/wiki/Roman_Palestine "Roman Palestine"), [Roman Egypt](/wiki/Roman_Egypt "Roman Egypt"), parts of [Asia Minor](/wiki/Asia_Minor "Asia Minor") and [Roman North Africa](/wiki/Africa_(Roman_province) "Africa (Roman province)"), while they entirely toppled the Sasanids. In the mid 7th century, following the [Muslim conquest of Persia](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Persia "Muslim conquest of Persia"), Islam penetrated into the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus") region, of which parts [would later](/wiki/Russo-Persian_Wars "Russo-Persian Wars") permanently become part of Russia.[[26]](#cite_note-27) This expansion of Islam continued under Umar's successors and then the [Umayyad Caliphate](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate"), which conquered the rest of Mediterranean North Africa and most of the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Visigothic_Kingdom "Visigothic Kingdom"). Over the next centuries Muslim forces were able to take further European territory, including [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus_in_the_Middle_Ages "Cyprus in the Middle Ages"), [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta"), [Septimania](/wiki/Septimania "Septimania"), [Crete](/wiki/Emirate_of_Crete "Emirate of Crete"), and [Sicily and parts of southern Italy](/wiki/History_of_Islam_in_southern_Italy "History of Islam in southern Italy").[[27]](#cite_note-28)

The Muslim conquest of Hispania began when the [Moors](/wiki/Moors "Moors") (mostly [Berbers](/wiki/Berber_people "Berber people") and some [Arabs](/wiki/Arab "Arab")) invaded the [Christian](/wiki/Christians "Christians") [Visigothic Kingdom](/wiki/Visigothic_Kingdom "Visigothic Kingdom") in the year 711, under their Berber leader [Tariq ibn Ziyad](/wiki/Tariq_ibn_Ziyad "Tariq ibn Ziyad"). They landed at [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar") on 30 April and worked their way northward. Tariq's forces were joined the next year by those of his superior, [Musa ibn Nusair](/wiki/Musa_ibn_Nusair "Musa ibn Nusair"). During the eight-year campaign most of the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") was brought under [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") rule—except for small areas in the north-northwest ([Asturias](/wiki/Asturias "Asturias")) and largely [Basque](/wiki/Basque_people "Basque people") regions in the [Pyrenees](/wiki/Pyrenees "Pyrenees"). This territory, under the Arab name [Al-Andalus](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus"), became part of the expanding [Umayyad](/wiki/Umayyad "Umayyad") empire.

The unsuccessful [second siege of Constantinople](/wiki/Siege_of_Constantinople_(717%E2%80%93718) "Siege of Constantinople (717–718)") (717) weakened the [Umayyad dynasty](/wiki/Umayyad "Umayyad") and reduced their prestige. After their success in overrunning Iberia, the conquerors moved northeast across the Pyrenees. They were defeated by the [Frankish](/wiki/Frankish_Empire "Frankish Empire") leader [Charles Martel](/wiki/Charles_Martel "Charles Martel") at the [Battle of Poitiers](/wiki/Battle_of_Tours "Battle of Tours") in 732. The Umayyads were overthrown in 750 by the [Abbāsids](/wiki/Abb%C4%81sids "Abbāsids") and most of the Umayyad clan were massacred.

A surviving Umayyad prince, [Abd-ar-rahman I](/wiki/Abd-ar-rahman_I "Abd-ar-rahman I"), escaped to Spain and founded a new Umayyad dynasty in the [Emirate of Cordoba](/wiki/Caliph_of_Cordoba "Caliph of Cordoba") in 756. Charles Martel's son [Pippin the Short](/wiki/Pippin_the_Short "Pippin the Short") retook [Narbonne](/wiki/Narbonne "Narbonne"), and his grandson Charlemagne established the [Marca Hispanica](/wiki/Marca_Hispanica "Marca Hispanica") across the Pyrenees in part of what today is [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia"), reconquering [Girona](/wiki/Girona "Girona") in 785 and [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona") in 801. The Umayyads in Hispania proclaimed themselves caliphs in 929.

### Birth of the Latin West

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Birth of the Latin West")]

Main article: [Barbarian kingdoms](/wiki/Barbarian_kingdoms "Barbarian kingdoms")

#### 700–850

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 700–850")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Sutton_Hoo_helmet_2016.png/250px-Sutton_Hoo_helmet_2016.png)](/wiki/File:Sutton_Hoo_helmet_2016.png)

The [Sutton Hoo helmet](/wiki/Sutton_Hoo_helmet "Sutton Hoo helmet"), an [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons") helmet from the early 7th century

Climatic conditions in Western Europe began to improve after 700.[[3]](#cite_note-beberglund-4)[[28]](#cite_note-29) In that year, the two major powers in western Europe were the [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks") in [Gaul](/wiki/Gaul "Gaul") and the [Lombards](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Lombards "Kingdom of the Lombards") in Italy.[[29]](#cite_note-Cantor,_Norman_p_102-30) The Lombards had been thoroughly Romanized, and [their kingdom](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Lombards "Kingdom of the Lombards") was stable and well developed. The Franks, in contrast, were barely any different from their barbarian Germanic ancestors. The [Kingdom of the Franks](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Franks "Kingdom of the Franks") was weak and divided.[[30]](#cite_note-31) Impossible to guess at the time, but by the end of the century, the Lombardic kingdom would be extinct, while the Frankish kingdom would have nearly reassembled the Western Roman Empire.[[29]](#cite_note-Cantor,_Norman_p_102-30)

Though much of Roman civilization north of the [Po River](/wiki/Po_(river) "Po (river)") had been wiped out in the years after the end of the Western Roman Empire, between the 5th and 8th centuries, new political and social infrastructure began to develop. Much of this was initially Germanic and pagan. [Arian Christian](/wiki/Arianism "Arianism") missionaries had been spreading Arian Christianity throughout northern Europe, though by 700 the religion of northern Europeans was largely a mix of [Germanic paganism](/wiki/Germanic_paganism "Germanic paganism"), Christianized paganism, and Arian Christianity.[[31]](#cite_note-Cantor,_Norman_p_147-32) [Chalcedonian Christianity](/wiki/Chalcedonian_Christianity "Chalcedonian Christianity") had barely started to spread in northern Europe by this time. Through the practice of [simony](/wiki/Simony "Simony"), local princes typically auctioned off ecclesiastical offices, causing priests and bishops to function as though they were yet another noble under the patronage of the prince.[[32]](#cite_note-33) In contrast, a network of [monasteries](/wiki/Christian_monasticism "Christian monasticism") had sprung up as monks sought separation from the world. These monasteries remained independent from local princes, and as such constituted the "church" for most northern Europeans during this time. Being independent from local princes, they increasingly stood out as centres of learning, of scholarship, and as religious centres where individuals could receive spiritual or monetary assistance.[[31]](#cite_note-Cantor,_Norman_p_147-32)

The interaction between the culture of the newcomers, their warband loyalties, the remnants of classical culture, and Christian influences, produced a new model for society, based in part on [feudal obligations](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism"). The centralized administrative systems of the Romans did not withstand the changes, and the institutional support for [chattel slavery](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery") largely disappeared. The [Anglo-Saxons](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons") in England had also started to convert from [Anglo-Saxon polytheism](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_paganism "Anglo-Saxon paganism") after the [arrival of Christian missionaries in 597](/wiki/Gregorian_mission "Gregorian mission").

#### Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Italy")]

Main articles: [Kingdom of Italy (medieval)](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy_(medieval) "Kingdom of Italy (medieval)") and [Italy in the Middle Ages](/wiki/Italy_in_the_Middle_Ages "Italy in the Middle Ages")

Further information: [Lombards](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards"), [King of Italy](/wiki/King_of_Italy "King of Italy"), and [Medieval Corsica](/wiki/Medieval_Corsica "Medieval Corsica")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Italien_zur_Langobardenzeit.jpg/330px-Italien_zur_Langobardenzeit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Italien_zur_Langobardenzeit.jpg)

The Lombard possessions in Italy: The Lombard Kingdom *(Neustria, Austria and Tuscia)* and the Lombard Duchies of Spoleto and Benevento

The Lombards, who first entered Italy in 568 under [Alboin](/wiki/Alboin "Alboin"), carved out a state in the north, with its capital at [Pavia](/wiki/Pavia "Pavia"). At first, they were unable to conquer the [Exarchate of Ravenna](/wiki/Exarchate_of_Ravenna "Exarchate of Ravenna"), the *[Ducatus Romanus](/wiki/Duchy_of_Rome "Duchy of Rome")*, and [Calabria](/wiki/Calabria "Calabria") and [Apulia](/wiki/Apulia "Apulia"). The next two hundred years were occupied in trying to conquer these territories from the Byzantine Empire.

The Lombard state was relatively Romanized, at least when compared to the Germanic kingdoms in northern Europe. It was highly decentralized at first, with the territorial dukes having practical sovereignty in their duchies, especially in the southern duchies of [Spoleto](/wiki/Duchy_of_Spoleto "Duchy of Spoleto") and [Benevento](/wiki/Duchy_of_Benevento "Duchy of Benevento"). For a decade following the death of [Cleph](/wiki/Cleph "Cleph") in 575, the Lombards did not even elect a king; this period is called the [Rule of the Dukes](/wiki/Rule_of_the_Dukes "Rule of the Dukes"). The first written legal code was composed in poor Latin in 643: the *[Edictum Rothari](/wiki/Edictum_Rothari "Edictum Rothari")*. It was primarily the codification of the oral legal tradition of the people.

The Lombard state was well-organized and stabilized by the end of the long reign of [Liutprand](/wiki/Liutprand,_King_of_the_Lombards "Liutprand, King of the Lombards") (717–744), but its collapse was sudden. Unsupported by the dukes, King [Desiderius](/wiki/Desiderius "Desiderius") was defeated and forced to surrender his kingdom to Charlemagne in 774. The Lombard kingdom ended and a period of Frankish rule was initiated. The Frankish king [Pepin the Short](/wiki/Pepin_the_Short "Pepin the Short") had, by the [Donation of Pepin](/wiki/Donation_of_Pepin "Donation of Pepin"), given the pope the "[Papal States](/wiki/Papal_States "Papal States")" and the territory north of that swath of papally-governed land was ruled primarily by Lombard and Frankish vassals of the [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor") until the rise of the city-states in the 11th and 12th centuries.

In the south, a period of chaos began. The [Duchy of Benevento](/wiki/Duchy_of_Benevento "Duchy of Benevento") maintained its sovereignty in the face of the pretensions of both the Western and Eastern Empires. In the 9th century, the [Muslims conquered Sicily](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Sicily "Muslim conquest of Sicily"). The cities on the [Tyrrhenian Sea](/wiki/Tyrrhenian_Sea "Tyrrhenian Sea") departed from Byzantine allegiance. Various states owing various nominal allegiances fought constantly over territory until events came to a head in the early 11th century with the coming of the [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans"), who conquered the whole of the south by the end of the century.

#### Britain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Britain")]

Main articles: [History of Anglo-Saxon England](/wiki/History_of_Anglo-Saxon_England "History of Anglo-Saxon England"), [History of Brittany](/wiki/History_of_Brittany "History of Brittany"), [History of Cornwall](/wiki/History_of_Cornwall "History of Cornwall"), [Scotland in the Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Scotland_in_the_Early_Middle_Ages "Scotland in the Early Middle Ages"), and [Wales in the Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Wales_in_the_Early_Middle_Ages "Wales in the Early Middle Ages")

[Roman Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain") was in a state of political and economic collapse at the time of the [Roman departure](/wiki/End_of_Roman_rule_in_Britain "End of Roman rule in Britain") c. 400. A [series of settlements](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_settlement_of_Britain "Anglo-Saxon settlement of Britain") (traditionally referred to as an invasion) by [Germanic peoples](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") began in the early fifth century, and by the sixth century the island would consist of many small kingdoms engaged in ongoing warfare with each other. The Germanic kingdoms are now collectively referred to as [Anglo-Saxons](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons"). Christianity began to take hold among the Anglo-Saxons in the sixth century, with 597 given as the traditional date for its large-scale adoption.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Gokstadskipet1.jpg/250px-Gokstadskipet1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gokstadskipet1.jpg)

The [Gokstad ship](/wiki/Gokstad_ship "Gokstad ship"), a 9th-century Viking [longship](/wiki/Longship "Longship"), excavated in 1882. Viking Ship Museum, Oslo, Norway

Western Britain ([Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales")), eastern and [northern Scotland](/wiki/Northern_Scotland "Northern Scotland") ([Pictland](/wiki/Picts "Picts")) and the [Scottish highlands](/wiki/Scottish_Highlands "Scottish Highlands") and [isles](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Scotland "List of islands of Scotland") continued their separate evolution. The [Irish](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people") descended and Irish-influenced people of western Scotland were Christian from the fifth century onward, the Picts adopted Christianity in the sixth century under the influence of [Columba](/wiki/Columba "Columba"), and the Welsh had been Christian since the Roman era.

The [Kingdom of Northumbria](/wiki/Northumbria "Northumbria") was the pre-eminent power c. 600–700, absorbing several weaker Anglo-Saxon and [Brythonic](/wiki/Britons_(Celtic_people) "Britons (Celtic people)") kingdoms, while [Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia") held a similar status c. 700–800. [Wessex](/wiki/Wessex "Wessex") would absorb all of the kingdoms in the south, both Anglo-Saxon and Briton. In Wales consolidation of power would not begin until the ninth century under the descendants of [Merfyn Frych](/wiki/Merfyn_Frych "Merfyn Frych") of [Gwynedd](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Gwynedd "Kingdom of Gwynedd"), establishing a hierarchy that would last until the [Norman invasion of Wales](/wiki/Norman_invasion_of_Wales "Norman invasion of Wales") in 1081.

The first [Viking](/wiki/Vikings "Vikings") raids on Britain began before 800, increasing in scope and destructiveness over time. In 865 a large, well-organized [Danish](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") Viking army (called the [Great Heathen Army](/wiki/Great_Heathen_Army "Great Heathen Army")) attempted a conquest, breaking or diminishing Anglo-Saxon power everywhere but in Wessex. Under the leadership of [Alfred the Great](/wiki/Alfred_the_Great "Alfred the Great") and his descendants, Wessex would at first survive, then coexist with, and eventually conquer the Danes. It would then establish the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") and rule until the establishment of an Anglo-Danish kingdom under [Cnut](/wiki/Cnut_the_Great "Cnut the Great"), and then again until the [Norman Invasion](/wiki/Norman_conquest_of_England "Norman conquest of England") of 1066.

Viking raids and invasion were no less dramatic for the north. Their defeat of the Picts in 839 led to a lasting [Norse](/wiki/Norway "Norway") heritage in northernmost Scotland, and it led to the combination of the Picts and [Gaels](/wiki/Gaels "Gaels") under the [House of Alpin](/wiki/House_of_Alpin "House of Alpin"), which became the [Kingdom of Alba](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Alba "Kingdom of Alba"), the predecessor of the [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland"). The Vikings combined with the Gaels of the [Hebrides](/wiki/Hebrides "Hebrides") to become the [Gall-Gaidel](/wiki/Norse%E2%80%93Gaels "Norse–Gaels") and establish the [Kingdom of the Isles](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Isles "Kingdom of the Isles").

#### Frankish Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Frankish Empire")]

Main articles: [Frankish Empire](/wiki/Frankish_Empire "Frankish Empire"), [Carolingian Empire](/wiki/Carolingian_Empire "Carolingian Empire"), and [Carolingian Renaissance](/wiki/Carolingian_Renaissance "Carolingian Renaissance")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Europe_814.svg/330px-Europe_814.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Europe_814.svg)

Europe in 814. Charlemagne's empire included most of modern France, Germany, [the Low Countries](/wiki/Benelux "Benelux"), Austria and northern Italy.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Sacre_de_Charlemagne.jpg/250px-Sacre_de_Charlemagne.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sacre_de_Charlemagne.jpg)

On 25 December 800, [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne") was crowned emperor by [Pope Leo III](/wiki/Pope_Leo_III "Pope Leo III").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Aix_dom_int_vue_cote.jpg/250px-Aix_dom_int_vue_cote.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aix_dom_int_vue_cote.jpg)

[Charlemagne's palace chapel](/wiki/Palatine_Chapel,_Aachen "Palatine Chapel, Aachen") in [Aachen](/wiki/Aachen "Aachen"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), now the central part of [Aachen Cathedral](/wiki/Aachen_Cathedral "Aachen Cathedral")

The [Merovingians](/wiki/Merovingian_dynasty "Merovingian dynasty") established themselves in the power vacuum of the former [Roman provinces](/wiki/Roman_province "Roman province") in Gaul, and [Clovis I](/wiki/Clovis_I "Clovis I") [converted to Christianity](/wiki/Conversion_to_Christianity "Conversion to Christianity") following his victory over the [Alemanni](/wiki/Alemanni "Alemanni") at the [Battle of Tolbiac](/wiki/Battle_of_Tolbiac "Battle of Tolbiac") (496), laying the foundation of the [Frankish Empire](/wiki/Francia "Francia"), the dominant state of early medieval Western [Christendom](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom"). The Frankish kingdom grew through a complex development of conquest, patronage, and alliance building. Due to [salic custom](/wiki/Salic_law "Salic law"), inheritance rights were absolute, and all land was [divided equally](/wiki/Partible_inheritance "Partible inheritance") among the sons of a dead land holder.[[33]](#cite_note-Cantor,_Norman_p_165-34) This meant that, when the king granted a prince land in reward for service, that prince and all of his descendants had an irrevocable right to that land that no future king could undo. Likewise, those princes (and their sons) could sublet their land to their own vassals, who could in turn sublet the land to lower sub-vassals.[[33]](#cite_note-Cantor,_Norman_p_165-34) This all had the effect of weakening the power of the king as his kingdom grew, since the result was that the land became controlled not just by more princes and vassals, but by multiple layers of vassals. This also allowed his nobles to attempt to build their own power base, though given the strict salic tradition of hereditary kingship, few would ever consider overthrowing the king.[[33]](#cite_note-Cantor,_Norman_p_165-34)

This increasingly fragmented arrangement was highlighted by [Charles Martel](/wiki/Charles_Martel "Charles Martel"), who as [Mayor of the Palace](/wiki/Mayor_of_the_Palace "Mayor of the Palace") was effectively the strongest prince in the kingdom.[[34]](#cite_note-Cantor,_Norman_p_189-35) His accomplishments were highlighted, not just by his famous defeat of invading Muslims at the [Battle of Tours](/wiki/Battle_of_Tours "Battle of Tours"), which is typically considered the battle that saved Europe from Muslim conquest, but by the fact that he greatly expanded Frankish influence. It was under his patronage that [Boniface](/wiki/Saint_Boniface "Saint Boniface") expanded Frankish influence into Germany by rebuilding the German church, with the result that, within a century, the German church was the strongest church in western Europe.[[34]](#cite_note-Cantor,_Norman_p_189-35) Yet despite this, Charles Martel refused to overthrow the Frankish king. His son, Pepin the Short, inherited his power, and used it to further expand Frankish influence. Unlike his father, however, Pepin decided to seize the Frankish kingship. Given how strongly Frankish culture held to its principle of inheritance, few would support him if he attempted to overthrow the king.[[35]](#cite_note-Cantor,_Norman_p_170-36) Instead, he sought the assistance of [Pope Zachary](/wiki/Pope_Zachary "Pope Zachary"), who was himself newly vulnerable due to fallout with the [Byzantine Emperor](/wiki/Byzantine_Emperor "Byzantine Emperor") over the [Iconoclastic Controversy](/wiki/Iconoclastic_Controversy "Iconoclastic Controversy"). Pepin agreed to support the pope and to give him land (the [Donation of Pepin](/wiki/Donation_of_Pepin "Donation of Pepin"), which created the [Papal States](/wiki/Papal_States "Papal States")) in exchange for being consecrated as the new Frankish king. Given that Pepin's claim to the kingship was now based on an authority higher than Frankish custom, no resistance was offered to Pepin.[[35]](#cite_note-Cantor,_Norman_p_170-36) With this, the Merovingian line of kings ended, and the [Carolingian](/wiki/Carolingian_dynasty "Carolingian dynasty") line began.

Pepin's son [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne") continued in the footsteps of his father and grandfather. He further expanded and consolidated the Frankish kingdom (now commonly called the [Carolingian Empire](/wiki/Carolingian_Empire "Carolingian Empire")). His reign also saw a cultural rebirth, commonly called the [Carolingian Renaissance](/wiki/Carolingian_Renaissance "Carolingian Renaissance"). Though the exact reasons are unclear, Charlemagne was crowned "Roman Emperor" by [Pope Leo III](/wiki/Pope_Leo_III "Pope Leo III") on Christmas Day, 800. Upon Charlemagne's death, his empire had united much of modern-day France, western Germany and northern Italy. The years after his death illustrated how Germanic his empire remained.[[35]](#cite_note-Cantor,_Norman_p_170-36) Rather than an orderly succession, his empire was divided in accordance with Frankish inheritance custom, which resulted in instability that plagued his empire until the last king of a united empire, [Charles the Fat](/wiki/Charles_the_Fat "Charles the Fat"), died in 887, which resulted in a permanent split of the empire into [West Francia](/wiki/West_Francia "West Francia") and [East Francia](/wiki/East_Francia "East Francia"). West Francia would be ruled by Carolingians until 987 and East Francia until 911, after which time the partition of the empire into France and Germany was complete.[[35]](#cite_note-Cantor,_Norman_p_170-36)

#### Feudalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Feudalism")]

Main articles: [Feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism") and [Manoralism](/wiki/Manoralism "Manoralism")

Around 800 there was a return to systematic agriculture in the form of the [open field](/wiki/Open_field_system "Open field system"), or strip, system. A [manor](/wiki/Manorialism "Manorialism") would have several fields, each subdivided into 1-acre (4,000 m2) strips of land. An acre measured one "furlong" of 220 yards by one "chain" of 22 yards (that is, about 200 m by 20 m). A furlong (from "furrow long") was considered to be the distance an ox could plough before taking a rest; the strip shape of the acre field also reflected the difficulty in turning early heavy ploughs. In the idealized form of the system, each family got thirty such strips of land. The three-field system of [crop rotation](/wiki/Crop_rotation "Crop rotation") was first developed in the 9th century: wheat or rye was planted in one field, the second field had a nitrogen-fixing crop, and the third was fallow.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

Compared to the earlier two-field system, a three-field system allowed for significantly more land to be put under cultivation. Even more important, the system allowed for two harvests a year, reducing the risk that a single crop failure will lead to famine. Three-field agriculture created a surplus of oats that could be used to feed horses. This surplus allowed for the replacement of the ox by the horse after the introduction of the padded [horse collar](/wiki/Horse_collar "Horse collar") in the 12th century. Because the system required a major rearrangement of real estate and of the social order, it took until the 11th century before it came into general use. The heavy wheeled plough was introduced in the late 10th century. It required greater animal power and promoted the use of teams of oxen. Illuminated manuscripts depict two-wheeled ploughs with both a mouldboard, or curved metal ploughshare, and a coulter, a vertical blade in front of the ploughshare. The Romans had used light, wheel-less ploughs with flat iron shares that often proved unequal to the heavy soils of northern Europe.

The return to systemic agriculture coincided with the introduction of a new social system called [feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism"). This system featured a hierarchy of reciprocal obligations. Each man was bound to serve his superior in return for the latter's protection. This made for confusion of territorial sovereignty since allegiances were subject to change over time and were sometimes mutually contradictory. Feudalism allowed the state to provide a degree of public safety despite the continued absence of bureaucracy and written records.

Manors became largely self-sufficient, and the volume of trade along long-distance routes and in market towns declined during this period, though never ceased entirely. Roman roads decayed and long-distance trade depended more heavily on water transport.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

### Viking Age

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Viking Age")]

Main article: [Viking Age](/wiki/Viking_Age "Viking Age")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Viking_Expansion.svg/500px-Viking_Expansion.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Viking_Expansion.svg)

* 9th century expansion
* 10th century expansion
