   [Viking raiding](/wiki/Viking_expansion "Viking expansion") regions

The Viking Age spans the period roughly between the late 8th and mid-11th centuries in [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") and [Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"), following the [Germanic Iron Age](/wiki/Germanic_Iron_Age "Germanic Iron Age") (and the [Vendel Age](/wiki/Vendel_Age "Vendel Age") in Sweden). During this period, the [Vikings](/wiki/Vikings "Vikings"), Scandinavian warriors and traders raided and explored most parts of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), [south-western Asia](/wiki/West_Asia "West Asia"), [northern Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa"), and [north-eastern North America](/wiki/L%27Anse_aux_Meadows "L'Anse aux Meadows").

With the means to travel (longships and open water), desire for goods led Scandinavian traders to explore and develop extensive trading partnerships in new territories. Some of the most important trading ports during the period include both existing and ancient cities such as [Aarhus](/wiki/Aarhus "Aarhus"), [Ribe](/wiki/Ribe "Ribe"), [Hedeby](/wiki/Hedeby "Hedeby"), [Vineta](/wiki/Vineta "Vineta"), [Truso](/wiki/Truso "Truso"), [Kaupang](/wiki/Kaupang "Kaupang"), [Birka](/wiki/Birka "Birka"), [Bordeaux](/wiki/Bordeaux "Bordeaux"), [York](/wiki/Jorvik "Jorvik"), [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin"), and [Aldeigjuborg](/wiki/Staraya_Ladoga "Staraya Ladoga").

Viking raiding expeditions were separate from, though coexisted with, regular trading expeditions. Apart from exploring Europe via its oceans and rivers, with the aid of their advanced navigational skills, they extended their trading routes across vast parts of the continent. They also engaged in warfare, looting and enslaving numerous Christian communities of medieval Europe for centuries, contributing to the development of feudal systems in Europe.

### Eastern Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Eastern Europe")]

600–1000

Main articles: [Slavic migrations to the Balkans](/wiki/Slavic_migrations_to_the_Balkans "Slavic migrations to the Balkans"), [Great Moravia](/wiki/Great_Moravia "Great Moravia"), [Duchy of Croatia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Croatia "Duchy of Croatia"), [Kingdom of Croatia (925–1102)](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Croatia_(925%E2%80%931102) "Kingdom of Croatia (925–1102)"), [Principality of Serbia (early medieval)](/wiki/Principality_of_Serbia_(early_medieval) "Principality of Serbia (early medieval)"), [Christianization of the Slavs](/wiki/Christianization_of_the_Slavs "Christianization of the Slavs"), [Western Turkic Khaganate](/wiki/Western_Turkic_Khaganate "Western Turkic Khaganate"), [Avar Khaganate](/wiki/Avar_Khaganate "Avar Khaganate"), [Khazar Khaganate](/wiki/Khazar_Khaganate "Khazar Khaganate"), [Old Great Bulgaria](/wiki/Old_Great_Bulgaria "Old Great Bulgaria"), and [Magyars](/wiki/Magyars "Magyars")

[Slavic tribes](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Slavic_tribes_in_the_7th_to_9th_century.jpg/250px-Slavic_tribes_in_the_7th_to_9th_century.jpg)](/wiki/File:Slavic_tribes_in_the_7th_to_9th_century.jpg)

[Slavic](/wiki/Early_Slavs "Early Slavs") tribes in central, eastern and southern Europe during the 7th to 9th centuries

---

In the beginning of the period, the [Slavic tribes](/wiki/Early_Slavs "Early Slavs") started to expand aggressively into Central and Southeastern Europe, including former Byzantine possessions in the Balkans. The first attested [Slavic](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs") polity was the so-called [Samo's Empire](/wiki/Samo%27s_Empire "Samo's Empire"), followed by [Great Moravia](/wiki/Great_Moravia "Great Moravia"), [Duchy of Bohemia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Bohemia "Duchy of Bohemia"), [Duchy of Croatia](/wiki/Duchy_of_Croatia "Duchy of Croatia") and [Serbia](/wiki/Principality_of_Serbia_(early_medieval) "Principality of Serbia (early medieval)"), also emerging under the aegis of the Frankish Empire in the early 9th century. Great Moravia was ultimately overrun by the [Magyars](/wiki/Magyars "Magyars"), who invaded the [Pannonian Basin](/wiki/Pannonian_Basin "Pannonian Basin") around 896. The Slavic state became a stage for confrontation between the Christian missionaries from Constantinople and Rome. Although West Slavic and most of the South Slavic lands since the beginning were under Roman ecclesiastical authority, the clergy of Constantinople succeeded in converting to Eastern Christianity two of the largest states of early medieval Europe, [Bulgaria](/wiki/First_Bulgarian_Empire "First Bulgarian Empire") (c. 864) and [Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%27 "Kievan Rus'") (c. 990), but also the Serbs (c. 870s).

The Early Middle Ages marked the beginning of the cultural distinctions between Western and Eastern Europe north of the Mediterranean. Influence from the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") impacted the Christianization and hence almost every aspect of the cultural and political development of the East from the preeminence of [Caesaropapism](/wiki/Caesaropapism "Caesaropapism") and [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity") to the spread of the [Cyrillic alphabet](/wiki/Cyrillic_alphabet "Cyrillic alphabet"). The turmoil of the so-called [Barbarian invasions](/wiki/Migration_Period "Migration Period") in the beginning of the period gradually gave way to more stabilized societies and states as the origins of contemporary Eastern Europe began to take shape during the [High Middle Ages](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages").

[Magyar tribes](/wiki/Magyar_tribes "Magyar tribes")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Kalandozasok.jpg/250px-Kalandozasok.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kalandozasok.jpg)

Magyar campaigns in the 10th century

---

  Magyar region

Turkic and Iranian invaders from [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") pressured the agricultural populations both in the Byzantine [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") and in Central Europe creating a number of successor states in the [Pontic steppes](/wiki/Pontic_steppe "Pontic steppe"). After the dissolution of the [Hunnic Empire](/wiki/Huns#Unified_Empire_under_Attila "Huns"), the [Western Turkic](/wiki/Western_Turkic_Khaganate "Western Turkic Khaganate") and [Avar Khaganates](/wiki/Avar_Khaganate "Avar Khaganate") dominated territories from [Pannonia](/wiki/Pannonian_Basin "Pannonian Basin") to the [Caspian Sea](/wiki/Caspian_Sea "Caspian Sea") before being replaced by the short lived [Old Great Bulgaria](/wiki/Old_Great_Bulgaria "Old Great Bulgaria") and the more successful [Khazar Khaganate](/wiki/Khazar_Khaganate "Khazar Khaganate") north of the Black Sea and the [Magyars](/wiki/Magyars "Magyars") in Central Europe.

The [Khazars](/wiki/Khazars "Khazars") were a nomadic Turkic people who managed to develop a multiethnic commercial state which owed its success to the control of much of the waterway trade between Europe and Central Asia. The Khazars also exacted tribute from the [Alani](/wiki/Alans "Alans"), [Magyars](/wiki/Magyars "Magyars"), various [Slavic](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs") tribes, the [Crimean Goths](/wiki/Crimean_Goths "Crimean Goths"), and the Greeks of [Crimea](/wiki/Crimea "Crimea"). Through a network of Jewish itinerant merchants, or [Radhanites](/wiki/Radhanites "Radhanites"), they were in contact with the trade emporia of India and Spain.

Once they found themselves confronted by [Arab expansionism](/wiki/Early_Muslim_conquests "Early Muslim conquests"), the Khazars pragmatically allied themselves with Constantinople and clashed with the [Caliphate](/wiki/Caliphate "Caliphate"). Despite initial setbacks, they managed to recover [Derbent](/wiki/Derbent "Derbent") and eventually penetrated as far south as [Caucasian Iberia](/wiki/Principality_of_Iberia "Principality of Iberia"), [Caucasian Albania](/wiki/Caucasian_Albania "Caucasian Albania") and [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia "Armenia"). In doing so, they effectively blocked the northward expansion of [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") into [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") even before [khan Tervel](/wiki/Khan_Tervel "Khan Tervel") achieved the same at the [Second Arab Siege of Constantinople](/wiki/Siege_of_Constantinople_(717%E2%80%9318) "Siege of Constantinople (717–18)") and several decades before the [Battle of Tours](/wiki/Battle_of_Tours "Battle of Tours") in Western Europe. Islam eventually penetrated into Eastern Europe in the 920s when [Volga Bulgaria](/wiki/Volga_Bulgaria "Volga Bulgaria") exploited the decline of Khazar power in the region to adopt Islam from the [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad") missionaries. The state religion of Khazaria, [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), disappeared as a political force with the fall of Khazaria, while Islam of Volga Bulgaria has survived in the region up to the present.

#### Bulgaria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Bulgaria")]

Main article: [First Bulgarian Empire](/wiki/First_Bulgarian_Empire "First Bulgarian Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/St._Theodor.jpg/250px-St._Theodor.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Theodor.jpg)

Ceramic icon of [St Theodore](/wiki/Theodore_the_Studite "Theodore the Studite") from around 900, found in [Preslav](/wiki/Preslav "Preslav"), Bulgarian capital from 893 to 972

In 632 the [Bulgars](/wiki/Bulgars "Bulgars") established the khanate of [Old Great Bulgaria](/wiki/Old_Great_Bulgaria "Old Great Bulgaria") under the leadership of [Kubrat](/wiki/Kubrat "Kubrat"). The Khazars managed to oust the Bulgars from Southern Ukraine into lands along middle [Volga](/wiki/Volga "Volga") ([Volga Bulgaria](/wiki/Volga_Bulgaria "Volga Bulgaria")) and along lower [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube") ([Danube Bulgaria](/wiki/First_Bulgarian_Empire "First Bulgarian Empire")).

In 681 the Bulgars founded a powerful and ethnically diverse state that played a defining role in the history of early medieval [Southeastern Europe](/wiki/Southeastern_Europe "Southeastern Europe"). Bulgaria withstood the pressure from [Pontic steppe](/wiki/Pontic_steppe "Pontic steppe") tribes like the [Pechenegs](/wiki/Pechenegs "Pechenegs"), [Khazars](/wiki/Khazars "Khazars"), and [Cumans](/wiki/Cumans "Cumans"), and in 806 destroyed the [Avar](/wiki/Avars_(Carpathians) "Avars (Carpathians)") Khanate. The Danube Bulgars were quickly slavicized and, despite constant campaigning against Constantinople, accepted Christianity from the Byzantine Empire. Through the efforts of missionaries [Cyril and Methodius](/wiki/Cyril_and_Methodius "Cyril and Methodius"), mainly their disciples like [Clement of Ohrid](/wiki/Clement_of_Ohrid "Clement of Ohrid") and [Naum](/wiki/Saint_Naum "Saint Naum"),[[38]](#cite_note-39) the spread, initially of the [Glagolitic](/wiki/Glagolitic_alphabet "Glagolitic alphabet"), and later of the [Cyrillic](/wiki/Cyrillic_script "Cyrillic script") alphabet, developed in the capital Preslav. The local vernacular dialect, now known as [Old Bulgarian](/wiki/Old_Bulgarian "Old Bulgarian") or Old Church Slavonic, was established as the language of books and liturgy among [Orthodox Christian](/wiki/Orthodoxy#Christianity "Orthodoxy") Slavs.

After the adoption of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") in 864, Bulgaria became a cultural and spiritual hub of the [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") Slavic world. The [Cyrillic script](/wiki/Cyrillic_script "Cyrillic script") was developed around 885–886, and was afterwards also introduced with books to [Serbia](/wiki/Principality_of_Serbia_(early_medieval) "Principality of Serbia (early medieval)") and [Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%27 "Kievan Rus'"). Literature, art, and architecture were thriving with the establishment of the [Preslav](/wiki/Preslav_Literary_School "Preslav Literary School") and [Ohrid Literary Schools](/wiki/Ohrid_Literary_School "Ohrid Literary School") along with the distinct Preslav Ceramics School. In 927 the [Bulgarian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Bulgarian_Orthodox_Church "Bulgarian Orthodox Church") was the first European national Church to gain independence with its own Patriarch while conducting services in the [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") [Old Church Slavonic](/wiki/Old_Church_Slavonic "Old Church Slavonic").

Under [Simeon I](/wiki/Simeon_I_of_Bulgaria "Simeon I of Bulgaria") (893–927), the state was the largest and one of the most powerful political entities of Europe, and it consistently threatened the existence of the Byzantine empire. From the middle of the 10th century Bulgaria was in decline as it entered a social and spiritual turmoil. It was in part due to Simeon's devastating wars, but was also exacerbated
by a series of successful Byzantine military campaigns. Bulgaria was conquered after a long resistance in 1018.

#### Kievan Rus'

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Kievan Rus'")]

Main article: [Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%27 "Kievan Rus'")

Led by a [Varangian](/wiki/Varangian "Varangian") dynasty, the [Kievan Rus'](/wiki/Kievan_Rus%27 "Kievan Rus'") controlled the [routes connecting Northern Europe to Byzantium](/wiki/Trade_route_from_the_Varangians_to_the_Greeks "Trade route from the Varangians to the Greeks") and to the Orient (for example: the [Volga trade route](/wiki/Volga_trade_route "Volga trade route")). The Kievan state began with the rule (882–912) of [Prince Oleg](/wiki/Oleg_of_Novgorod "Oleg of Novgorod"), who extended his control from [Novgorod](/wiki/Veliky_Novgorod "Veliky Novgorod") southwards along the [Dnieper](/wiki/Dnieper "Dnieper") river valley in order to protect trade from [Khazar](/wiki/Khazars "Khazars") incursions from the east and moved his capital to the more strategic [Kiev](/wiki/Kiev "Kiev"). [Sviatoslav I](/wiki/Sviatoslav_I_of_Kiev "Sviatoslav I of Kiev") (died 972) achieved the first major expansion of Kievan Rus' territorial control, fighting a war of conquest against the [Khazar Empire](/wiki/Khazar_Empire "Khazar Empire") and inflicting a serious blow on [Bulgaria](/wiki/First_Bulgarian_Empire "First Bulgarian Empire"). A [Rus' attack](/wiki/Sviatoslav%27s_invasion_of_Bulgaria "Sviatoslav's invasion of Bulgaria") (967 or 968), instigated by the Byzantines, led to the collapse of the Bulgarian state and the occupation of the east of the country by the Rus'. An ensuing [direct military confrontation between the Rus' and Byzantium](/wiki/Rus%27%E2%80%93Byzantine_War_(970%E2%80%93971) "Rus'–Byzantine War (970–971)") (970–971) ended with a [Byzantine victory](/wiki/Siege_of_Dorostolon "Siege of Dorostolon") (971). The Rus' withdrew and the Byzantine Empire incorporated eastern Bulgaria. Both before and after their [conversion to Christianity](/wiki/Christianization_of_Kievan_Rus%27 "Christianization of Kievan Rus'") (conventionally dated 988 under [Vladimir I of Kiev](/wiki/Vladimir_I_of_Kiev "Vladimir I of Kiev")—known as Vladimir the Great), the Rus' also embarked on predatory military campaigns against the Byzantine Empire, some of which resulted in trade treaties. The importance of Russo-Byzantine relations to Constantinople was highlighted by the fact that Vladimir I of Kiev, son of [Svyatoslav I](/wiki/Sviatoslav_I "Sviatoslav I"), became the only foreigner to marry (989) a [Byzantine princess](/wiki/Anna_Porphyrogenita "Anna Porphyrogenita") of the [Macedonian dynasty](/wiki/Macedonian_dynasty "Macedonian dynasty") (which ruled the [Eastern Roman Empire](/wiki/Eastern_Roman_Empire "Eastern Roman Empire") from 867 to 1056), a singular honour sought in vain by many other rulers.

## Transmission of learning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Transmission of learning")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Silos-Claustro.jpg/250px-Silos-Claustro.jpg)](/wiki/File:Silos-Claustro.jpg)

[Abbey of Santo Domingo de Silos](/wiki/Abbey_of_Santo_Domingo_de_Silos "Abbey of Santo Domingo de Silos"). In the Early Middle Ages, cultural life was concentrated at [monasteries](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery").

With the [fall of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire") and with urban centres in decline, [literacy](/wiki/Literacy "Literacy") and learning decreased in the West.[[39]](#cite_note-Cantor,_Norman_p_52-40) [De-urbanization](/wiki/Counterurbanization "Counterurbanization") reduced the scope of education, and by the [6th century](/wiki/6th_century "6th century") teaching and learning moved to monastic and cathedral schools, with the study of biblical texts at the centre of education.[[40]](#cite_note-41) The education of the [laity](/wiki/Laity "Laity") continued with little interruption in Italy, Spain, and the southern part of [Gaul](/wiki/Gaul "Gaul"), where Roman influences lasted longer. In the 7th century, however, learning expanded in Ireland and the Celtic lands, where Latin was a foreign language and Latin texts were eagerly studied and taught.[[41]](#cite_note-42) The [Carolingian Renaissance](/wiki/Carolingian_Renaissance "Carolingian Renaissance") of [classical education](/wiki/Classical_education "Classical education") appeared in the [Carolingian Empire](/wiki/Carolingian_Empire "Carolingian Empire") in the [8th century](/wiki/8th_century "8th century").

In the [Eastern Roman Empire](/wiki/Eastern_Roman_Empire "Eastern Roman Empire") (Byzantium), learning (in the sense of formal education involving literature) was maintained at a higher level than in the West. The classical education system, which would persist for hundreds of years, emphasized [grammar](/wiki/Grammar "Grammar"), [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek"), and [rhetoric](/wiki/Rhetoric "Rhetoric"). Pupils read and reread classic works and wrote essays imitating their style. By the 4th century, this education system was [Christianized](/wiki/Christianized "Christianized"). In *[De Doctrina Christiana](/wiki/De_doctrina_Christiana "De doctrina Christiana")* (started 396, completed 426), [Augustine](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo") explained how classical education fits into the Christian worldview: Christianity is a religion of the book, so Christians must be literate. [Tertullian](/wiki/Tertullian "Tertullian") was more skeptical of the value of classical learning, asking "What indeed has Athens to do with Jerusalem?"[[42]](#cite_note-43)

### Science

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Science")]

Main article: [History of science § Middle Ages](/wiki/History_of_science#Middle_Ages "History of science")

In the ancient world, Greek was the primary language of science. Advanced scientific research and teaching was mainly carried on in the [Hellenistic](/wiki/Hellenistic "Hellenistic") side of the Roman empire, and in Greek. Late Roman attempts to translate Greek writings into Latin had limited success.[[43]](#cite_note-44) As the knowledge of Greek declined, the Latin West found itself cut off from some of its Greek philosophical and scientific roots. For a time, Latin-speakers who wanted to learn about science had access to only a couple of books by [Boethius](/wiki/Boethius "Boethius") (c. 470–524) that summarized Greek handbooks by [Nicomachus of Gerasa](/wiki/Nicomachus_of_Gerasa "Nicomachus of Gerasa"). [Isidore of Seville](/wiki/Isidore_of_Seville "Isidore of Seville") produced a Latin encyclopedia in 630. Private libraries existed, and monasteries also kept various kinds of texts.

The study of nature was pursued more for practical reasons than as an abstract inquiry: the need to care for the sick led to the study of medicine and of ancient texts on drugs;[[44]](#cite_note-45) the need for monks to determine the proper time to pray led them to study the motion of the stars;[[45]](#cite_note-46) and the need to compute the [date of Easter](/wiki/Date_of_Easter "Date of Easter") led them to study and teach mathematics and the motions of the Sun and Moon.[[46]](#cite_note-47)[[47]](#cite_note-48)

### Carolingian Renaissance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Carolingian Renaissance")]

Main article: [Carolingian Renaissance](/wiki/Carolingian_Renaissance "Carolingian Renaissance")

In the late 8th century, there was renewed interest in [Classical Antiquity](/wiki/Classical_Antiquity "Classical Antiquity") as part of the Carolingian Renaissance. Charlemagne carried out a reform in [education](/wiki/Education "Education"). The English monk [Alcuin of York](/wiki/Alcuin "Alcuin") elaborated a project of scholarly development aimed at resuscitating classical knowledge by establishing programs of study based upon the seven [liberal arts](/wiki/Liberal_arts "Liberal arts"): the *trivium*, or literary education ([grammar](/wiki/Grammar "Grammar"), [rhetoric](/wiki/Rhetoric "Rhetoric"), and [dialectic](/wiki/Dialectic "Dialectic")), and the *quadrivium*, or scientific education ([arithmetic](/wiki/Arithmetic "Arithmetic"), [geometry](/wiki/Geometry "Geometry"), [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy"), and [music](/wiki/Music "Music")). From 787 on, [decrees](/wiki/Decree "Decree") began to circulate recommending the restoration of old schools and the founding of new ones across the empire.

Institutionally, these new schools were either under the responsibility of a [monastery](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery") ([monastic schools](/wiki/Monastic_school "Monastic school")), a [cathedral](/wiki/Cathedral "Cathedral"), or a [noble court](/wiki/Noble_court "Noble court"). The teaching of [dialectic](/wiki/Dialectic "Dialectic") (a discipline that corresponds to today's [logic](/wiki/Logic "Logic")) was responsible for the increase in the interest in speculative inquiry; from this interest would follow the rise of the [Scholastic](/wiki/Scholasticism "Scholasticism") tradition of [Christian philosophy](/wiki/Christian_philosophy "Christian philosophy"). In the 12th and 13th centuries, many of those schools founded under the auspices of Charlemagne, especially [cathedral schools](/wiki/Cathedral_school "Cathedral school"), would become [universities](/wiki/Medieval_university "Medieval university").

### Byzantium's golden age

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Byzantium's golden age")]

[Macedonian Byzantium](/wiki/Byzantium_under_the_Macedonians "Byzantium under the Macedonians")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Paris_psaulter_gr139_fol1v.jpg/250px-Paris_psaulter_gr139_fol1v.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paris_psaulter_gr139_fol1v.jpg)

Miniature from the [Paris Psalter](/wiki/Paris_Psalter "Paris Psalter")


---

[Byzantium in the 10th century](/wiki/Byzantium_under_the_Macedonians "Byzantium under the Macedonians") experienced a wide-scale cultural revival.

Byzantium's great intellectual achievement was the [Corpus Juris Civilis](/wiki/Corpus_Juris_Civilis "Corpus Juris Civilis") ("Body of Civil Law"), a massive compilation of [Roman law](/wiki/Roman_law "Roman law") made under [Justinian](/wiki/Justinian "Justinian") (r. 528–565). The work includes a section called the *[Digesta](/wiki/Pandects "Pandects")* which abstracts the principles of Roman law in such a way that they can be applied to any situation. The level of literacy was considerably higher in the Byzantine Empire than in the Latin West. Elementary education was much more widely available, sometimes even in the countryside. Secondary schools still taught the *[Iliad](/wiki/Iliad "Iliad")* and other classics.

As for higher education, the [Neoplatonic Academy](/wiki/Neoplatonic_Academy "Neoplatonic Academy") in [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens") was closed in 526. There was also a school in Alexandria which remained open until the Arab conquest (640). The [University of Constantinople](/wiki/University_of_Constantinople "University of Constantinople"), founded by Emperor [Theodosius II](/wiki/Theodosius_II "Theodosius II") (425), seems to have dissolved around this time. It was refounded by Emperor [Michael III](/wiki/Michael_III "Michael III") in 849. Higher education in this period focused on rhetoric, although [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle")'s logic was covered in simple outline. Under the [Macedonian dynasty](/wiki/Macedonian_dynasty "Macedonian dynasty") (867–1056), Byzantium enjoyed a golden age and a revival of classical learning. There was little original research, but many lexicons, anthologies, encyclopedias, and commentaries.

### Islamic learning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Islamic learning")]

In the course of the 11th century, Islam's scientific knowledge began to reach Western Europe, via Islamic Spain. The works of [Euclid](/wiki/Euclid "Euclid") and [Archimedes](/wiki/Archimedes "Archimedes"), lost in the West, were translated from Arabic to Latin in Spain. The modern [Hindu–Arabic numeral system](/wiki/Hindu%E2%80%93Arabic_numeral_system "Hindu–Arabic numeral system"), including a notation for zero, were developed by Hindu mathematicians in the 5th and 6th centuries. Muslim mathematicians learned of it in the 7th century and added a notation for decimal fractions in the 9th and 10th centuries. Around 1000, Gerbert of Aurillac (later [Pope Sylvester II](/wiki/Pope_Sylvester_II "Pope Sylvester II")) made an [abacus](/wiki/Abacus "Abacus") with counters engraved with [Arabic numerals](/wiki/Arabic_numerals "Arabic numerals"). A treatise by [Al-Khwārizmī](/wiki/Al-Khw%C4%81rizm%C4%AB "Al-Khwārizmī") on how to perform calculations with these numerals was translated into Latin in Spain in the 12th century.

### Monasteries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Monasteries")]

[Monasteries](/wiki/Monasteries "Monasteries") were targeted in the eighth and ninth centuries by [Vikings](/wiki/Viking "Viking") who invaded the coasts of northern Europe. They were targeted not only because they stored books but also precious objects that were looted by invaders. In the earliest monasteries, there were no special rooms set aside as a library, but from the sixth century onwards libraries became an essential aspect of monastic life in Western Europe. The [Benedictines](/wiki/Benedictines "Benedictines") placed books in the care of a librarian who supervised their use. In some monastic reading rooms, valuable books would be chained to shelves, but there were also lending sections as well. Copying was also another important aspect of monastic libraries, this was undertaken by resident or visiting monks and took place in the *[scriptorium](/wiki/Scriptorium "Scriptorium")*. In the Byzantine world, religious houses rarely maintained their own copying centres. Instead they acquired donations from wealthy donors. In the tenth century, the largest collection in the Byzantine world was found in the monasteries of [Mount Athos](/wiki/Mount_Athos "Mount Athos") (modern-day Greece), which accumulated over 10,000 books. Scholars travelled from one monastery to another in search of the texts they wished to study. Travelling monks were often given funds to buy books, and certain monasteries which held a reputation for intellectual activities welcomed travelling monks who came to copy manuscripts for their own libraries. One of these was the [monastery of Bobbio](/wiki/Monastery_of_Bobbio "Monastery of Bobbio") in Italy, which was founded by the Irish abbot [Columbanus](/wiki/Columbanus "Columbanus") in 614, and by the ninth century boasted a catalogue of 666 manuscripts, including religious works, classical texts, histories and mathematical treatises.[[48]](#cite_note-49)

## Christianity West and East

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Christianity West and East")]

Main article: [Christianity in the Middle Ages](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_Middle_Ages "Christianity in the Middle Ages")

Further information: [Christianity in the 6th century](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_6th_century "Christianity in the 6th century"), [Christianity in the 7th century](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_7th_century "Christianity in the 7th century"), and [Christianity in the 8th century](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_8th_century "Christianity in the 8th century")

[Medieval Christians](/wiki/Medieval_Christians "Medieval Christians")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Sacr_Gelasianum_131v_132.jpg/250px-Sacr_Gelasianum_131v_132.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sacr_Gelasianum_131v_132.jpg)

[Sacramentarium Gelasianum](/wiki/Sacramentarium_Gelasianum "Sacramentarium Gelasianum").

---

Frontispiece of Incipit from the Vatican manuscript

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/St_Boniface_-_Baptising-Martyrdom_-_Sacramentary_of_Fulda_-_11Century.jpg/250px-St_Boniface_-_Baptising-Martyrdom_-_Sacramentary_of_Fulda_-_11Century.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Boniface_-_Baptising-Martyrdom_-_Sacramentary_of_Fulda_-_11Century.jpg)

[St Boniface](/wiki/St_Boniface "St Boniface") – Baptism and Martyrdom.

From the [early Christians](/wiki/Early_Christians "Early Christians"), early medieval Christians inherited a church united by major creeds, a stable [Biblical canon](/wiki/Biblical_canon "Biblical canon"), and a well-developed philosophical tradition. The [history of medieval Christianity](/wiki/History_of_medieval_Christianity "History of medieval Christianity") traces Christianity during the Middle Ages—the period after the fall of the Western Roman Empire until the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"). The institutional structure of Christianity in the west during this period is different from what it would become later in the Middle Ages. As opposed to the later church, the church of the Early Middle Ages consisted primarily of the monasteries.[[49]](#cite_note-Cantor,_Norman_p_153-50) The practice of [simony](/wiki/Simony "Simony") has caused the ecclesiastical offices to become the property of local princes, and as such the monasteries constituted the only church institution independent of the local princes. In addition, the [papacy](/wiki/Papacy "Papacy") was relatively weak, and its power was mostly confined to central Italy.[[49]](#cite_note-Cantor,_Norman_p_153-50) Individualized religious practice was uncommon, as it typically required membership in a religious order, such as the [Order of Saint Benedict](/wiki/Order_of_Saint_Benedict "Order of Saint Benedict").[[49]](#cite_note-Cantor,_Norman_p_153-50) Religious orders would not proliferate until the high Middle Ages. For the typical Christian at this time, religious participation was largely confined to occasionally receiving mass from wandering monks. Few would receive this as often as once a month.[[49]](#cite_note-Cantor,_Norman_p_153-50) By the end of this period, individual practice of religion was becoming more common, as monasteries started to transform into something approximating modern churches, where some monks might even give occasional sermons.[[49]](#cite_note-Cantor,_Norman_p_153-50)

During the Early Middle Ages, the divide between Eastern and Western Christianity widened, paving the way for the [East-West Schism](/wiki/East-West_Schism "East-West Schism") in the 11th century. In the West, the power of the [Bishop of Rome](/wiki/Bishop_of_Rome "Bishop of Rome") expanded. In 607, [Boniface III](/wiki/Boniface_III "Boniface III") became the first Bishop of Rome to use the title [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Pope Gregory I](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_I "Pope Gregory I") used his office as a temporal power, expanded Rome's missionary efforts to the British Isles, and laid the foundations for the expansion of monastic orders. Roman church traditions and practices gradually replaced local variants, including [Celtic Christianity](/wiki/Celtic_Christianity "Celtic Christianity") in the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"). Various barbarian tribes went from raiding and pillaging the island to invading and settling. They were entirely pagan, having never been part of the Empire, though they experienced Christian influence from the surrounding peoples, such as those who were converted by the mission of [Augustine of Canterbury](/wiki/Augustine_of_Canterbury "Augustine of Canterbury"), sent by [Pope Gregory I](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_I "Pope Gregory I"). In the East, the conquests of Islam reduced the power of the Greek-speaking [patriarchates](/wiki/Patriarchates "Patriarchates").

### Christianization of the West

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Christianization of the West")]

Main article: [Christianization](/wiki/Christianization "Christianization")

The [Roman Church](/wiki/State_church_of_the_Roman_Empire "State church of the Roman Empire"), the only centralized institution to survive the [fall of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire") intact, was the sole unifying cultural influence in the West, preserving Latin learning, maintaining the art of writing, and preserving a centralized administration through its network of [bishops](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") ordained in succession. In Europe, a formalized institution of marriage was established.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The proscribed degree of [consanguinity](/wiki/Consanguinity "Consanguinity") varied, but the custom made marriages annullable by application to the Pope.[[50]](#cite_note-51) The Early Middle Ages are characterized by the urban control of bishops and the territorial control exercised by dukes and counts. The rise of [urban communes](/wiki/Medieval_commune "Medieval commune") marked the beginning of the [High Middle Ages](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages").

The [Christianization of Germanic tribes](/wiki/Germanic_Christianity "Germanic Christianity") began in the 4th century with the Goths and continued throughout the Early Middle Ages, led in the 6th to 7th centuries by the [Hiberno-Scottish mission](/wiki/Hiberno-Scottish_mission "Hiberno-Scottish mission") and replaced in the 8th to 9th centuries by the [Anglo-Saxon mission](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_mission "Anglo-Saxon mission"), with Anglo-Saxons like [Alcuin](/wiki/Alcuin "Alcuin") playing an important role in the [Carolingian Renaissance](/wiki/Carolingian_Renaissance "Carolingian Renaissance"). [Boniface](/wiki/Saint_Boniface "Saint Boniface"), the Apostle of the Germans, propagated Christianity in the Frankish Empire during the 8th century. He helped shape Western Christianity, and many of the dioceses he proposed remain until today. After his martyrdom, he was quickly hailed as a saint. By 1000, even [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland") had become Christian, leaving only more remote parts of Europe ([Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia"), the [Baltic](/wiki/Baltic_peoples "Baltic peoples"), and [Finnic](/wiki/Finnic_peoples "Finnic peoples") lands) to be Christianized during the High Middle Ages.

## Europe in 1000

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Europe in 1000")]

Further information: [AD 1000](/wiki/AD_1000 "AD 1000")

While the assumption that Europeans feared the world would end in the year 1000 is popular today, such beliefs were largely confined to a few select monasteries in France.[[51]](#cite_note-52) Ordinary clerks used [regnal years](/wiki/Regnal_year "Regnal year"), e.g. the 4th year of the reign of Robert II (the Pious) of France. The use of the modern "anno domini" system of dating was largely confined to chroniclers of universal history, such as the [Venerable Bede](/wiki/Venerable_Bede "Venerable Bede").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Western Europe remained less developed compared to the Islamic world, with its vast network of caravan trade, or China, at this time the world's most populous empire under the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty"). [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") had a population of about 300,000, but Rome had a mere 35,000 and Paris 20,000.[[52]](#cite_note-53)[[53]](#cite_note-54) By contrast, [Córdoba](/wiki/C%C3%B3rdoba,_Spain "Córdoba, Spain"), in Islamic Spain, at this time the world's largest city contained 450,000 inhabitants. The [Vikings](/wiki/Vikings "Vikings") had a trade network in northern Europe, including a [route connecting the Baltic to Constantinople](/wiki/Trade_route_from_the_Varangians_to_the_Greeks "Trade route from the Varangians to the Greeks") through Russia, as did the [Radhanites](/wiki/Radhanites "Radhanites").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/St_Michaels_Church_Hildesheim.jpg/250px-St_Michaels_Church_Hildesheim.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Michaels_Church_Hildesheim.jpg)

[St. Michael's Church, Hildesheim](/wiki/St._Michael%27s_Church,_Hildesheim "St. Michael's Church, Hildesheim"), Germany, 1010s. [Ottonian architecture](/wiki/Ottonian_architecture "Ottonian architecture") draws its inspiration from [Carolingian](/wiki/Carolingian_architecture "Carolingian architecture") and Byzantine architecture.

With nearly the entire nation freshly ravaged by the Vikings, England was in a desperate state. The long-suffering English later responded with a massacre of Danish settlers in 1002, leading to a round of reprisals and finally to Danish rule (1013), though England regained independence shortly after. Christianization made rapid progress and proved itself the long-term solution to the problem of barbarian raiding. The territories of Scandinavia were soon to be fully Christianized Kingdoms: [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") in the 10th century, [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") in the 11th, and [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), the country with the least raiding activity, in the 12th. [Kievan Rus](/wiki/Kievan_Rus "Kievan Rus"), recently converted to Orthodox Christianity, flourished as the largest state in Europe. Iceland, [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland"), and [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") were all declared Christian about 1000.

North of Italy, where masonry construction was never extinguished, stone construction was replacing timber in important structures. Deforestation of the densely wooded continent was under way. The 10th century marked a return of urban life, with the Italian cities doubling in population. [London](/wiki/London "London"), abandoned for many centuries, was again England's main economic centre by 1000. By 1000, [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges") and [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent") held regular trade fairs behind castle walls, a tentative return of economic life to western Europe.

In the culture of Europe, several features surfaced soon after 1000 that mark the end of the Early Middle Ages: the rise of the [medieval communes](/wiki/Medieval_commune "Medieval commune"), the reawakening of city life, and the appearance of the [burgher class](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie"), the founding of the first [universities](/wiki/Medieval_university "Medieval university"), the rediscovery of [Roman law](/wiki/Roman_law "Roman law"), and the beginnings of vernacular literature.

In 1000, the papacy was firmly under the control of German Emperor [Otto III](/wiki/Otto_III "Otto III"), or "emperor of the world" as he styled himself. But later church reforms enhanced its independence and prestige: the [Cluniac movement](/wiki/Abbey_of_Cluny "Abbey of Cluny"), the building of the first great Transalpine stone cathedrals and the collation of the mass of accumulated [decretals](/wiki/Decretal "Decretal") into a formulated [canon law](/wiki/Canon_law "Canon law"). Meanwhile, a new Central European power was taking shape, and on [Christmas Day](/wiki/Christmas_Day "Christmas Day") of 1000, [Stephen I](/wiki/Stephen_I_of_Hungary "Stephen I of Hungary") was crowned as the first [king of Hungary](/wiki/King_of_Hungary "King of Hungary"), stabilising the country, which had been ruled by [grand princes](/wiki/Grand_Prince_of_the_Hungarians "Grand Prince of the Hungarians"). Like the [Civitas Schinesghe](/wiki/Civitas_Schinesghe "Civitas Schinesghe"), which was also consolidating at the time, the country was seen as a promising political and trading partner, and a *federati* (ally) by the emperor.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

### Rise of Islam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Rise of Islam")]

Main articles: [Spread of Islam](/wiki/Spread_of_Islam "Spread of Islam"), [Early Muslim conquests](/wiki/Early_Muslim_conquests "Early Muslim conquests"), and [Rashidun Caliphate](/wiki/Rashidun_Caliphate "Rashidun Caliphate")

Rise of Islam

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Arabische_Rijk.jpg/250px-Arabische_Rijk.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arabische_Rijk.jpg)

Arab expansion in the 7th century

* *Area I* : Muhammad
* *Area II* : Abu Bakr

The rise of Islam begins around the time [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad") and his followers took flight, the [Hijra](/wiki/Hijra_(Islam) "Hijra (Islam)"), from [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca "Mecca") to the city of [Medina](/wiki/Medina "Medina"). Muhammad spent his last ten years in a [series of battles to conquer the Arabian region](/wiki/Military_career_of_Muhammad "Military career of Muhammad"). From 622 to 632, Muhammad as the leader of a Muslim community in Medina was engaged in a state of war with the Meccans. In the proceeding decades, the area of [Basra](/wiki/Basra "Basra") was conquered by the Muslims. During the reign of [Umar](/wiki/Umar "Umar"), the [Muslim army](/wiki/Rashidun_army "Rashidun army") found it a suitable place to construct a base. Later the area was settled and a mosque was erected. [Madyan](/wiki/Midian "Midian") was conquered and settled by Muslims, but the environment was considered harsh and the settlers moved to [Kufa](/wiki/Kufa "Kufa"). Umar defeated the rebellion of several Arab tribes in a successful campaign, unifying the entire Arabian peninsula and giving it stability. Under [Uthman](/wiki/Uthman "Uthman")'s leadership, the empire, through the [Muslim conquest of Persia](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Persia "Muslim conquest of Persia"), expanded into [Fars](/wiki/Fars_province "Fars province") in 650, some areas of [Khorasan](/wiki/Greater_Khorasan "Greater Khorasan") in 651, and the conquest of Armenia was begun in the 640s. In this time, the [Rashidun Caliphate](/wiki/Rashidun_Caliphate "Rashidun Caliphate") extended over the whole Sassanid Persian Empire and to more than two-thirds of the Eastern Roman Empire. The [First Fitna](/wiki/First_Fitna "First Fitna"), or the First Islamic Civil War, lasted for the entirety of [Ali ibn Abi Talib](/wiki/Ali_ibn_Abi_Talib "Ali ibn Abi Talib")'s reign. After the recorded peace treaty with [Hassan ibn Ali](/wiki/Hassan_ibn_Ali "Hassan ibn Ali") and the suppression of early [Kharijites](/wiki/Kharijites "Kharijites")' disturbances, [Muawiyah I](/wiki/Muawiyah_I "Muawiyah I") acceded to the position of Caliph.

### Islamic expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Islamic expansion")]

Main article: [Umayyad Caliphate](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate")

[Muslim Expansions in 7th & 8th Centuries](/wiki/Early_Muslim_conquests "Early Muslim conquests")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Map_of_expansion_of_Caliphate.svg/250px-Map_of_expansion_of_Caliphate.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_expansion_of_Caliphate.svg)

The Islamic expansion of the 7th and 8th centuries

* Muhammad's conquests, 622–632
* Rashidun Caliphate, 632–661
* Umayyad Caliphate, 661–750

The [Muslim conquests](/wiki/Early_Muslim_conquests "Early Muslim conquests") of the [Eastern Roman Empire and Arab wars](/wiki/Byzantine%E2%80%93Arab_Wars "Byzantine–Arab Wars") occurred between 634 and 750. Starting in 633, Muslims [conquered Iraq](/wiki/Islamic_conquest_of_Iraq "Islamic conquest of Iraq"). The [Muslim conquest of Syria](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Syria "Muslim conquest of Syria") would begin in 634 and would be complete by 638. The [Muslim conquest of Egypt](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Egypt "Muslim conquest of Egypt") started in 639. Before the [Muslim invasion of Egypt](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Egypt "Muslim conquest of Egypt") began, the Eastern Roman Empire had already lost the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant") and its Arab ally, the [Ghassanid Kingdom](/wiki/Ghassanids "Ghassanids"), to the Muslims. The Muslims would bring Alexandria under control and the fall of Egypt would be complete by 642. Between 647 and 709, [Muslims swept across North Africa](/wiki/Umayyad_conquest_of_North_Africa "Umayyad conquest of North Africa") and established their authority over that region.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Spain_Andalusia_Cordoba_BW_2015-10-27_13-54-14.jpg/250px-Spain_Andalusia_Cordoba_BW_2015-10-27_13-54-14.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spain_Andalusia_Cordoba_BW_2015-10-27_13-54-14.jpg)

The 10th-century [Grand Mosque of Cordoba](/wiki/Mezquita "Mezquita"), [Córdoba, Spain](/wiki/C%C3%B3rdoba,_Spain "Córdoba, Spain")

The site of the Grand Mosque was originally a pagan temple, then a Visigothic Christian church, before the Umayyad Moors at first converted the building into a mosque and then built a new mosque on the site.

The [Transoxiana](/wiki/Transoxiana "Transoxiana") region was conquered by [Qutayba ibn Muslim](/wiki/Qutayba_ibn_Muslim "Qutayba ibn Muslim") between 706 and 715 and loosely held by the Umayyads from 715 to 738. This conquest was consolidated by [Nasr ibn Sayyar](/wiki/Nasr_ibn_Sayyar "Nasr ibn Sayyar") between 738 and 740. It was under the Umayyads from 740 to 748 and under the Abbasids after 748. [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh"), attacked in 664, would be subjugated by 712. Sindh became the easternmost province of the Umayyad. The Umayyad conquest of [Hispania](/wiki/Hispania "Hispania") ([Visigothic Spain](/wiki/Visigothic_Spain "Visigothic Spain")) would begin in 711 and end by 718. The [Moors](/wiki/Moors "Moors"), under [Al-Samh ibn Malik](/wiki/Al-Samh_ibn_Malik "Al-Samh ibn Malik"), swept up the Iberian peninsula and by 719 overran [Septimania](/wiki/Septimania "Septimania"); the area would fall under their full control in 720. With the [Islamic conquest of Persia](/wiki/Islamic_conquest_of_Persia "Islamic conquest of Persia"), the Muslim subjugation of the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus") would take place between 711 and 750. The end of the sudden Islamic Caliphate expansion ended around this time. The final Islamic dominion eroded the areas of the Iron Age Roman Empire in the Middle East and controlled strategic areas of the Mediterranean.

At the end of the 8th century, the former Western Roman Empire was decentralized and overwhelmingly rural. The [Islamic conquest and rule of Sicily and Malta](/wiki/History_of_Islam_in_southern_Italy "History of Islam in southern Italy") was a process which started in the 9th century. Islamic rule over Sicily was effective from 902, and the complete rule of the island lasted from 965 until 1061. The Islamic presence on the Italian Peninsula was ephemeral and limited mostly to semi-permanent soldier camps.

### Caliphs and empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Early_Middle_Ages&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Caliphs and empire")]

Main articles: [Abbasid Caliphate](/wiki/Abbasid_Caliphate "Abbasid Caliphate") and [Islamic Golden Age](/wiki/Islamic_Golden_Age "Islamic Golden Age")

The [Abbasid Caliphate](/wiki/Abbasid_Caliphate "Abbasid Caliphate"), ruled by the [Abbasid dynasty](/wiki/Abbasid_dynasty "Abbasid dynasty") of caliphs, was the third of the Islamic caliphates. Under the Abbasids, the [Islamic Golden Age](/wiki/Islamic_Golden_Age "Islamic Golden Age") philosophers, scientists, and engineers of the Islamic world contributed enormously to technology, both by preserving earlier traditions and by adding their own inventions and innovations. Scientific and intellectual achievements blossomed in the period.

The Abbasids built their capital in Baghdad after replacing the Umayyad caliphs from all but the Iberian peninsula. The influence held by Muslim merchants over African-Arabian and Arabian-Asian trade routes was tremendous. As a result, Islamic civilization grew and expanded on the basis of its merchant economy, in contrast to their Christian, Indian, and Chinese peers who built societies from an agricultural landholding nobility.

The Abbasids flourished for two centuries but slowly went into decline with the rise to power of the Turkish army they had created, the [Mamluks](/wiki/Mamluks "Mamluks"). Within 150 years of gaining control of Persia, the caliphs were forced to cede power to local dynastic emirs who only nominally acknowledged their authority. After the Abbasids lost their military dominance, the Iranian [Samanids](/wiki/Samanids "Samanids") (or Samanid Empire) rose up in Central Asia.
